chapters.title: If insight is magic, we are magicians
chapters.text:
George Land, a general systems scientist, prepared a creativity test to assist NASA in hiring talent. It became so successful that in 1968 he offered it to 1,600 five-year-old kids. A huge 98% of them passed it with flying colors. When the children reached age ten, the researcher reassessed their skills. This time 30% passed the test. At age 15, participants sat for the test again and only 12% did well. As George Land concluded, we are born inventive. Yet the more we master non-creative behavior, the less we think outside the box.

Is this replacement irreversible? The answer depends on our beliefs. If we only wait for creativity or insights, the chances of getting them are slim. Instead, we should realize that these skills result from lateral thinking. Its main goals are questioning patterns and generating new ideas.

Insight isn't a miracle. It's a consequence of restructuring the pattern.

The need to weaken some assumptions doesn't mean they are wrong or unnecessary. Our logical thinking is an indispensable tool for our survival. It's inconceivable to categorize the world “from scratch” daily. Yet patterns have some drawbacks. One of the most crucial ones — they limit how we view things. With time, we get used to a particular way of dealing with problems or a route for driving and keep other options on margin. However, the conventional approach doesn't equate to the most effective one.

Like logical thinking lateral thinking is a way of using the mind. ~ Edward de Bono, Dr.

The good news is that we can overcome these limitations. The following sections will show you how.

chapters.title: Mistakes ignite lateral thinking
chapters.text:
Usually, we have little room for mistakes. We get a bad mark, punishment, or lose money if we do something wrong. Failure has become one of the most prevalent fears, and we strive to be constantly correct. Typically, it means strictly adhering to the rules, which blocks lateral thinking. We dismiss all ideas that aren't “proper” as ridiculous.

However, insights depend on wrong ideas. In essence, mistakes are non-standard ways of dealing with situations. Thus, they can undermine the entrenched pattern, offering another perspective.

Instead of disregarding “strange” suggestions, we should explore them. Mistakes are fertile soil for inventiveness.

Thus, it's better to ask yourself the following questions when you have an atypical idea:

• What is the driving force behind it?
• What knowledge gap does it reveal?

A few mistakes, and we will grasp a fresh approach to the task. Then we can consciously apply it and reach a beneficial result. To facilitate adjusting to a new way of thinking, we can divide our workspace into parts. One is for writing down all our ideas; another is for evaluating them and analyzing the errors.

For instance, some mistakes are helpful because they reveal illusory limitations. Let's consider this case. One company sells bottles of brandy with a whole pear inside. How do they do this? The most popular answer is that manufacturers add the fruit and then the bottleneck or bottom. The desire to understand how the company squeezed a whole pear in is a false limitation. In fact, manufacturers place the head of the bottle over a branch with a bud and wait until nature does its job.

How can we overcome the inertia of these basic assumptions? It's sound to analyze the conclusions we take for granted. If we unintentionally narrow the task or choose the wrong starting point, we must search for alternative ways to deal with a puzzle.

Did you know? Humor and insight rest on the transformation of a pattern. For the former, the change is temporal, while for the latter is permanent. Recall the moments when you suddenly came up with a brilliant idea. Even though it wasn't hilarious, you probably smiled.

chapters.title: Don't let the first solution stop you
chapters.text:
The pillar of lateral thinking is freedom from absolute correctness. It states that any solution, no matter how effective, is only one of the possible options. There are other, more practical ones. This assumption differentiates lateral thinking from logical.

Using the latter, we search until we find the “right” answer. Applying a creative approach, we seek alternatives even after reaching a solution. Some of the options are more beneficial, and some less. Yet all gradually weaken our patterns, allowing us to think outside the box. It can play a crucial role when we have a significant project or interview with the company of our dreams.

It's reasonable to train the “muscle” of lateral thinking beforehand. Then, even in a high-pressure environment, we can leverage our creative potential.

Set a minimum number of ways (three to five) to make generating alternatives a habit. Anytime you can come up with more — grasp the opportunity. For instance, the task is to turn a square into four equal pieces. The following are creative options:

• To form two equal rectangles out of a square and divide them into equal triangles.
• To split the figure into sixteen equal squares and unite them into four L shapes.

Another way to master searching for alternatives is by offering various interpretations. For example, we can take a photo from the news and speculate about an event. If it shows people on the beach, we may assume it's a swimming contest. Yet the real intention is a forthcoming protest against the poor state of the territory. If you can't find a suitable picture, you may cover or blur some of its parts.

Additionally, we can read an article and retell it from the viewpoints of different people. Suppose it's news about a kid who won the contest for young inventors. In that case, we can speculate how the child who won, the participant who got second place, and the organizers judge this event. There are always a few ways to approach a situation. The following section is about how to find them.

chapters.title: Innovative ideas need new connections
chapters.text:
Logical thinking is intentional: we have a goal and look for relevant guidance. Consequently, we always pay attention to facts that confirm our pattern. Lateral thinking helps us avoid this trap. It uses information for nothing but its effect. Thus, even random data can be a stimulus to look at the problem from an unexpected angle.

We are geniuses at creating patterns. If we intentionally put two unrelated things in focus, soon, the brain will find a new connection between them.

What can give impetus to non-standard ideas? We already apply some of these techniques: discussing the problem with friends who specialize in different topics or going to an exhibition. Still, in these cases, we are tempted to mull over the same issue or focus on items related to our task. Therefore, it's better to plan our random triggers for innovative propositions.

We can start with analogies. For instance, compare searching for keys to orienting yourself on a foggy day. How can methods from the former assist the latter?

Then, we may proceed to stimuli without an apparent connection to our problem. Before looking at the task, write a page of the dictionary, the line, and the word. It will be a random stimulus. Another way is to utilize objects around us; for instance, opt for “the nearest yellow thing.” No matter how far from the task the chosen word or object is, we should work with it.

Often random stimuli lead to others associated with the issue. For example, the task is a new design for a watch, and the chosen word is “coffee.” We may recall a reusable cup, focus on different occasions we have drunk from it, and devise a hand watch for utilizing it in various situations. As a result, the invented model can transform into a fashionable pendant. Also, we may use one word, such as a “leaf," to resolve a few problems; for instance, how to learn faster and store information better.

The critical element of establishing a new pattern is our confidence to find the connection. Its secret is simple: the more we practice, the more self-reliant we become.

chapters.title: Explore techniques to facilitate fresh suggestions
chapters.text:
When the pattern becomes entrenched, it's difficult for ideas to spring into our minds. The following methods aim to remove those obstacles.

Scrutinize your starting point: in childhood, we resolved a puzzle about one fish and three fishermen. Their tangled lines impeded seeing which fisherman was the lucky one. Our gut feeling prompted us to start with the fish. This idea is a telling example that sometimes we should begin from the opposite end.

It can be a critical factor in a successful result. For example, a team received a task to eliminate weeds and used particular herbicides. Was it the best solution? After farmers applied the chemicals, the territory had no weeds, yet the harvest was less bountiful. At the same time, a field on which these substances weren't used had unwanted plants, but the crop yields were typical. The team instead should have focused on the main goal — improving the harvest.

If the objective comprises many factors, it's beneficial to list them all and gradually consider each.

Divide the task artificially: a solution that has stood the test of time is complicated to improve; we can't imagine how to do it differently. Therefore, we may break it into unusual parts and then reassemble it. For instance, the goal is to pick apples. Instead of shaking, gathering, and transporting, we may look at the task from the following perspectives:

• The role of the tree
• The role of the fruit
• The role of the machine

At first sight, it may seem awkward. Yet the “role of a tree” incentivized people to change how an apple tree grew, which made obtaining the fruit easier.

Another method is to break a task in two. Then we divide each part into two, and so on. Components may cover only part of the task; the main aim is to discover novel connections.

Apply the reversal procedure: sometimes when we can't reach the aim, we may devise a plan to make the goal come to us. A famous fable will serve as a source of inspiration. Once, when a bird wanted to drink, it found a jug. Yet the water was so close to the bottom of the jug that it was impossible to reach. So, instead of trying to reach the water at the bottom of the pitcher, the bird started to put pebbles inside. Soon the level of the water rose.

chapters.title: “Impossible” and other words that block creativity
chapters.text:
Patterns frequently hide not in basic assumptions or starting points but in our language. The catch is that our brain is an avid fan of polarization. There is rarely room for semitones, categorizing things as applicable or irrelevant. It is a sophisticated system of thousands of separate drawers. When the idea is a part of one pattern, the brain hardly uses it for another.

Thus, the first way to identify limitations is to pay attention to the categories we utilize. Events or objects constantly fall into groups with labels like “beneficial,” “unstable,” and “strange.” In the previous section, we mentioned a puzzle about tangled fishing lines. Some kids feel they should follow the one with the fish but decline this idea, calling it ”cheating.“ And as a result, they spend more time on the situation than those who treat the insight as a ”brilliant solution."

In discussion, asking what others mean by “fair” or “effective” is helpful.

Secondly, analyzing which pronoun we use more, “I” or “me,” may help to reveal our patterns. If you use “I” more, try the following exercise: jot down about your day and rewrite the text without using the “I” pronoun. You will see how many other factors impact your daily routine. This task is also suitable for those who prefer the “me” form: eliminate this pronoun in the new version.

Thirdly, note the particle “not” and words like “impossible,” “irrelevant,” and “meaningless.” They are signposts of entrenched patterns. If someone says “water flows uphill,” others will likely disagree, saying it's impossible. Yet in nature, this phenomenon does, in fact, exist; for instance, the reverse waterfall: strong wind makes the water flow upwards.

Moreover, we can use a new category. Edward de Bono coined a particular term for this tool of lateral thinking — PO. It indicates to others that our suggestion to seek more ideas isn't a denial of someone's input but a way to train creative skills. For example, we may say, “PO: water can flow up” or “PO: we can get the energy from leaf fall.”

chapters.title: Tips to make the most of a brainstorming session
chapters.text:
The fruitfulness of brainstorming depends on the organization of the process. The following are recommendations on how to avoid pitfalls.

The size of the group. It's beneficial to have a team of 6-15 people. In this case, the conversation won't get too personal, and everyone has enough time to express themselves.

Time. Twenty minutes is a sound choice. People should leave the discussion full of insights; this will make them associate creative tasks with success. Awkward silence, on the contrary, may undermine confidence.

The atmosphere. A lot rests on the atmosphere if this method is new for people.

Brainstorming participants should feel that this meeting is a unique territory where novelty thrives; therefore, it's a safe place to speak out and be themselves.

How you create a particular atmosphere varies due to the age of the participants. For example, the door to the room for kids' brainstorming can have a poster, “Kingdom of inventiveness.” We may also add a signpost that says, on entering, we must leave all negative comments (“bad,” “old,” and so on) behind. These steps are essential to let participants feel relaxed and focus on the task, not on worries.

The problem. It should be narrow enough; otherwise, suggestions won’t have anything in common. The latter is a cornerstone of brainstorming: different angles are incentives for participants' creativity, so following comments should spring from previous ones. The possible themes for discussion are a new design of money and ways of cooling buildings.

A problem is simply the difference between what one has and what one wants. ~ Edward de Bono, Dr.

Saving ideas. A notetaker should write down all the opinions, even if they partly coincide. It's better to check the list with what was said regularly. The chairperson can help by asking people to summarize their suggestions.

Role of the chairman. This participant should do the following:

• Eliminate any sign of evaluation.
• Check that two people don't speak simultaneously.
• Fill moments of silence with propositions.

Evaluation session. We can do this on a different day and in a larger group. All participants should classify ideas into the following categories:

• Practical solution
• Unusual approach
• Confirmation of gap in knowledge

This categorization will demonstrate that even the most irrelevant idea contains a grain of innovative solution.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Lateral thinking is as essential as logical, and they should work cooperatively. Yet often, we concentrate only on the latter, forgetting that along with immense benefits, it has limitations. The former compensates for them. Thus, creative skills benefit anybody who wants to master their mind.

Non-standard thinking aims to unlock the most effective solutions for dealing with problems or using things. It rests on a few principles:

• Suspend evaluation to free the way for new ideas and patterns.
• Use information for the sake of its effect. Say whatever crosses your mind, use remote analogies, random words, or objects, and divide tasks into unexpected components.
• Generate alternatives. Finding a reasonable solution shouldn't stop us from continuing the search. The more options we discover, the better, since each may become a breakthrough or a tool to reassess the whole task.

Lateral thinking techniques also weaken the power of patterns. They question the starting point, chosen limitations, and the accuracy of the aim.

Thinking outside the box isn't a panacea. Yet it assists in broadening your outlook and discovering opportunities you've never thought of. In the age of automation, it can become your ticket to a higher position on a career ladder or to start a business. New mindset skills open novel opportunities.

Try this

• ‌Schedule time for practicing lateral thinking to make it a habit.
• ‌Pay attention to how the inner voice's evaluation blocks the way for new ideas. Train to postpone judgment of suggestions.
• ‌Watch your favorite comedy show and analyze how jokes challenge patterns. Humor also stems from creativity.

Read it till the end?
